title: Thermodynamic incompleteness in non-Markovian Majorana transport I: Island dynamics and missing transport statistics
abstract: We show that the complete knowledge of the non-Markovian island-state dynamics of a floating Majorana island does not, in general, determine the thermodynamic transport statistics measured in the leads. We demonstrate this statement in a Coulomb-blockaded island with $M$ Majorana zero modes coupled to structured reservoirs. In the cotunneling regime, a Schrieffer-Wolff transformation gives reservoir-assisted transitions generated by Majorana bilinears. After the reservoirs are traced out, the island state determines the memory kernel associated with each bilinear, and this is enough to predict all island-state observables within the cotunneling approximation. It is not enough to determine which lead or detector channel supplied the electron, absorbed the electron, or carried the corresponding energy exchange. This is a genuine loss of thermodynamic information, not an error in the island equation. We formulate the result as a thermodynamic completeness criterion: an island memory equation determines a transport observable only when that observable is constant over all assignments of reservoir channels that give the same island memory kernel. The criterion gives a measurable prediction. Two structured-reservoir Majorana devices can have identical island-state tomography and relaxation, but different charge noise measured separately in the leads, heat noise, and mixed charge-energy correlations. The geometry of the projection from reservoir records to island kernels and the topology of the network of tunnel contacts identify which transport information is absent from island-state dynamics.
summary: This paper demonstrates that the non-Markovian dynamics of a Majorana island do not contain enough information to fully determine the transport statistics of its connected leads. It establishes a thermodynamic completeness criterion and shows that different reservoir configurations can produce identical island-state evolution while yielding different heat and charge noise. This highlights a fundamental loss of information when tracing out environmental degrees of freedom.
main_problem: Investigating whether the non-Markovian dynamics of a Majorana island are sufficient to reconstruct the full thermodynamic transport statistics (charge, heat, and noise) measured in the connected leads.
main_result: The author proves that island-state dynamics are thermodynamically incomplete because the memory kernel discards the specific identity of reservoir channels, meaning two devices with identical island relaxation can exhibit different transport noise.
method: The study uses a Schrieffer-Wolff transformation to derive a cotunneling Hamiltonian, a Born memory expansion for the non-Markovian master equation, and the counting field method to track transport statistics.
model_or_system: A floating superconducting Majorana island in the Coulomb blockade regime, featuring M Majorana zero modes coupled to structured reservoirs via tunneling contacts.
key_observables: Charge transfer, heat transfer, charge noise, heat noise, and mixed charge-energy correlations.
important_parameters: Coulomb charging energy (Ec), gate voltage (ng), cotunneling regime, and Majorana bilinear operators.
main_assumptions: The analysis is restricted to the weak-cotunneling regime and assumes Gaussian reservoirs and analytic counting-field generating functionals.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of thermodynamic incompleteness, defines the Majorana island model in the cotunneling regime, presents a completeness theorem and a geometric/topological criterion for reconstructibility, and concludes with experimental predictions and limitations.
why_interesting: It provides a fundamental limit on the reconstructibility of open quantum system dynamics, specifically showing that observing the system's internal state is insufficient to infer the full thermodynamic record of its environment.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
